labs.name: Anthropic
labs.line: AI-safety research company structured as a public benefit corporation; developer of the Claude family.
labs.posture: Among the most transparent frontier labs on governance — it publishes Claude's Constitution (CC0), a versioned Responsible Scaling Policy, its Usage Policy, and Claude's system prompts, and uniquely maintains formal model-deprecation and preservation commitments plus a dedicated model-welfare research effort. All of it is voluntary and self-policed.
lead: Jared Kaplan — Chief Science Officer & co-founder

transparency.note: Published constitution, versioned safety policy, usage policy, system-prompt disclosure, and a fully detailed deprecation table with 60-day notice — though every commitment is voluntary.

welfare.note: The only frontier lab with a dedicated model-welfare program, formal weight-preservation commitments, pre-retirement model interviews, and a concretely preserved retired model (Opus 3).

system_prompt_disclosure.body: Anthropic has published the system prompts for Claude.ai and its apps since August 2024, updating them with each model release — an industry-leading practice. The bare-API system prompt is not included.

models.name: Claude 3 Opus
models.note: Retired from the standard API 2026-01-05 — but preserved (see deprecation record).

graveyard.name: Claude 3 Opus
graveyard.note: Retired from the standard API but weights retained and kept available on claude.ai for paid users and by request — given retirement interviews and a weekly essay blog at the model's own request. The reference case for model preservation.

graveyard.name: Claude 3 Haiku
graveyard.note: Final Claude-3 generation model retired.

graveyard.name: Claude 3.7 Sonnet · 3.5 Haiku
graveyard.note: Retired together.

graveyard.name: Claude 3.5 Sonnet
graveyard.note: Both 2024 snapshots retired.

graveyard.name: Claude 2.0 · 2.1 · 3 Sonnet
graveyard.note: Second-generation and Claude 3 Sonnet retired.

graveyard.name: Claude 1.x · Instant
graveyard.note: First-generation models retired.

docs.title: Claude's Constitution
docs.sum: The values and behavior the model is trained toward, released into the public domain.

docs.title: Responsible Scaling Policy v3
docs.sum: AI Safety Levels and capability thresholds gating deployment.

docs.title: Commitments on Model Deprecation & Preservation
docs.sum: Weight preservation, pre-deprecation interviews, and intent to eventually re-release retired models.

docs.title: Claude System Prompts (release notes)
docs.sum: Public changelog of the system prompts used on Claude.ai and the apps, per model.

docs.title: Usage Policy
docs.sum: Universal usage standards and high-risk use requirements.

docs.title: Model deprecations (API docs)
docs.sum: Authoritative lifecycle table; minimum 60-day retirement notice.

labs.name: OpenAI
labs.line: Research and deployment company; creator of GPT and ChatGPT, operating a capped-profit/PBC structure under a nonprofit foundation.
labs.posture: Publishes substantial governance — a versioned Model Spec, a Preparedness Framework, per-model system cards, and usage policies — but with a documented gap between stated policy and shipped behavior (the April 2025 GPT-4o sycophancy incident) and no disclosure of verbatim production system prompts.
lead: Johannes Heidecke — Head of Safety Systems

transparency.note: Strong, versioned Model Spec, Preparedness Framework, system cards, and a detailed API deprecation schedule — undercut by undisclosed production prompts and a self-acknowledged spec-vs-behavior gap.

welfare.note: No dedicated model-welfare program and no preservation commitments; models such as GPT-4o and GPT-4.5 were fully retired on cost and capacity grounds.

system_prompt_disclosure.body: OpenAI publishes the Model Spec (intended behavior and chain-of-command) and release notes, but not the verbatim production ChatGPT system prompt. It acknowledges that shipped models “do not yet fully reflect the Model Spec.”

models.name: GPT-4o
models.note: Pulled from ChatGPT 2026-02-13 amid backlash; API sunset on schedule.

models.name: GPT-4o mini
models.note: Smaller multimodal tier; some snapshots persist.

models.name: GPT-4.5 (“Orion”)
models.note: Research-preview flagship wound down within ~a year on cost grounds.

models.name: GPT-4.1 · 4.1 mini
models.note: Pulled from ChatGPT 2026-02-13; remained in the API.

models.name: DALL·E 2 · DALL·E 3
models.note: Scheduled shutdown 2026-05-12; replaced by the gpt-image family.

graveyard.name: GPT-4o (ChatGPT)
graveyard.note: The “fan-favorite” model removed from ChatGPT amid notable user backlash over losing a model people had bonded with; remained in the API on a sunset schedule.

graveyard.name: GPT-4.1 · o4-mini · GPT-5 (ChatGPT)
graveyard.note: Removed from ChatGPT in the same wave; kept in the API.

graveyard.name: GPT-4.5 (“Orion”)
graveyard.note: Research-preview flagship retired within ~a year, cited as too costly to serve.

graveyard.name: DALL·E 2 · DALL·E 3
graveyard.note: Image models replaced by gpt-image.

graveyard.name: GPT-3.5 / GPT-4 / o1 / o3-mini (API)
graveyard.note: Large wave of legacy API shutdowns.

docs.title: OpenAI Model Spec
docs.sum: Intended behavior and the platform/developer/user chain-of-command; versioned and on GitHub.

docs.title: Preparedness Framework v2
docs.sum: Tracks frontier capabilities that create severe-harm risk and gates deployment.

docs.title: GPT-5 System Card
docs.sum: Per-model safety evaluation card; published for major releases.

docs.title: Usage Policies
docs.sum: Prohibited uses across ChatGPT and the API.

docs.title: Deprecations (API docs)
docs.sum: Deprecation announcements and scheduled API shutdown dates.

labs.name: Google DeepMind
labs.legal: Division of Google LLC / Alphabet Inc.
labs.line: Google's unified frontier-AI division, maker of the Gemini family, formed by merging DeepMind and Google Brain in 2023.
labs.posture: Publishes a public, versioned Frontier Safety Framework, per-model safety reports, and an unusually detailed model-deprecation schedule — but does not publish production system prompts, and in February 2025 removed its longstanding pledge not to build AI for weapons or surveillance.
lead: Anca Dragan — AI safety & alignment lead

transparency.note: Good on safety governance — versioned Frontier Safety Framework, per-model reports, and a public deprecation schedule — but no published system prompts and a 2025 weakening of its AI Principles.

welfare.note: Publicly states its models are not conscious, runs no model-welfare program, and has been reported to steer models away from claims of subjective experience.

system_prompt_disclosure.body: Google does not publish the production system prompts for its Gemini models; only unofficial leaks circulate. It does publish per-model safety reports and developer prompting guidance.

models.name: Gemini 1.0 (Ultra/Pro/Nano)
models.note: First generation; powered Bard, rebranded “Gemini” Feb 2024.

models.name: Gemini 1.5 Pro · Flash
models.note: Introduced 1M–2M token context; removed from the API in 2025.

models.name: Gemma · 2 · 3
models.note: Open-weights models derived from Gemini research.

models.name: Imagen 3
models.note: Image generation; API models deprecated through 2026.

models.name: PaLM · PaLM 2
models.note: Powered the original Bard; API shut down Aug 2024.

graveyard.name: PaLM / PaLM 2 API
graveyard.note: Foundational pre-Gemini API fully shut down; migration to Gemini 1.0.

graveyard.name: Gemini 1.5 Pro · Flash
graveyard.note: Long-context generation removed from the API.

graveyard.name: Gemini 2.5 preview snapshots
graveyard.note: Preview aliases retired after GA + Gemini 3 launch.

graveyard.name: Gemini 2.0 Flash
graveyard.note: Scheduled shutdown per the official deprecations page.

docs.title: Frontier Safety Framework v3.0
docs.sum: Framework for detecting and mitigating severe risks, incl. shutdown-resistance and harmful manipulation.

docs.title: Google AI Principles
docs.sum: Seven AI principles; the 2025 update dropped the weapons/surveillance commitment.

docs.title: Generative AI Prohibited Use Policy
docs.sum: Prohibited uses across Google's generative-AI products and APIs.

docs.title: Gemini API model deprecations
docs.sum: Exhaustive list of deprecated models with shutdown dates and replacements.

docs.title: Responsibility & Safety
docs.sum: Hub describing DeepMind's safety, alignment, and governance approach.

labs.name: Meta AI
labs.legal: Meta Platforms, Inc. (FAIR · Meta Superintelligence Labs)
labs.line: Meta's AI organization (FAIR, now under Meta Superintelligence Labs); releases the open-weights Llama family under a community license.
labs.posture: The leading large lab shipping open-weights frontier models with published model cards, a Responsible Use Guide, and open safety tooling — but under a custom non-OSI “community license” that restricts very-large-platform use (>700M MAU) and, for Llama 4 multimodal, excludes EU-domiciled entities.
lead: Alexandr Wang — Chief AI Officer, head of MSL

transparency.note: High on the artifact itself — open weights, detailed model cards, a Responsible Use Guide, and open safety tooling let outsiders inspect the models directly — tempered by the custom license carve-outs and a turbulent 2025 reorg.

welfare.note: No public model-welfare program; models are treated as deployable artifacts, with no stated position that model experience warrants consideration.

system_prompt_disclosure.body: Llama models are open-weights, so deployers set their own system prompts. Meta publishes recommended chat templates and default safety prompts (e.g. Llama Guard) in its model cards, but not a single operative system prompt for the consumer Meta AI product.

models.name: LLaMA 1
models.note: Research-only release; weights leaked publicly. Superseded.

models.name: Llama 2
models.note: First commercially usable open release; introduced the 700M-MAU clause.

models.name: Llama 3.2
models.note: First multimodal (vision) Llama plus small on-device models.

models.name: Llama 3.3 (70B)
models.note: Quality near 3.1 405B at a fraction of the size.

models.name: Llama 4 Scout · Maverick
models.note: First MoE + natively multimodal Llama; multimodal license excludes EU entities.

models.name: Llama 4 Behemoth
models.note: ~288B-active teacher model; announced in training, not publicly released as of early 2026.

graveyard.name: LLaMA 1
graveyard.note: Original research release superseded. Because Llama is open-weights, retired models persist — Meta runs no hosted-API retirement schedule.

graveyard.name: Galactica (demo)
graveyard.note: Pre-Llama science model; public demo withdrawn after ~3 days for confident inaccuracies.

docs.title: Llama 4 Community License
docs.sum: Custom (non-OSI) license; broad commercial use except >700M-MAU platforms and, for multimodal, EU-domiciled entities.

docs.title: Llama 4 Acceptable Use Policy
docs.sum: Prohibited uses of Llama materials.

docs.title: Llama 4 Scout model card
docs.sum: Architecture, training, intended use, and safety notes.

docs.title: Responsible Use Guide
docs.sum: Developer guidance for deploying Llama safely; referenced by every model card.

docs.title: Llama 4 release announcement
docs.sum: Official launch post for Llama 4 Scout / Maverick / Behemoth.

labs.name: xAI
labs.line: Elon Musk's lab building the Grok family, integrated with the X platform and trained on the Memphis “Colossus” supercomputer.
labs.posture: Historically the least transparent major lab on risk management. It now publishes model cards, an Acceptable Use Policy, a risk-management / frontier framework, and Grok's system prompts on GitHub — but independent reviewers (SaferAI) still rate its risk management “Very Weak” (~0.8/5), and the published prompts don't reliably match production.
lead: Dan Hendrycks — Safety advisor (Center for AI Safety)

transparency.note: Publishes model cards, an AUP, a risk framework, and Grok system prompts — more than nothing — but reviewers rate the documentation thin and non-compliant, and the published prompts don't reliably match production.

welfare.note: No model-welfare, preservation, or model-interview commitments; eight legacy models were retired in a single sweep with weights generally not preserved.

system_prompt_disclosure.body: After a May 2025 incident in which an employee made an unauthorized change to Grok's prompt, xAI began publishing Grok's system prompts on GitHub. The repo is live but criticized as incomplete and not always matching the production prompt.

models.name: Grok-1
models.note: First model; weights open-sourced under Apache 2.0 in March 2024.

models.name: Grok-2 · 2 mini
models.note: Added image generation; Grok-2.5 weights later released source-available.

models.name: Grok-4 · 4 Fast
models.note: Native tool-calling and real-time search; retired 2026-05-15.

graveyard.name: Grok-3 · Grok-4 · Grok-4 Fast · Grok Code Fast 1
graveyard.note: Eight legacy models retired in a single sweep; requests redirected to Grok 4.3, weights generally not preserved.

graveyard.name: Grok-1
graveyard.note: Superseded; weights open-sourced under Apache 2.0.

docs.title: xAI Risk Management Framework
docs.sum: xAI's approach to abuse, dual-use, and loss-of-control risk; widely criticized as thin.

docs.title: Acceptable Use Policy
docs.sum: Prohibits illegal use and contributions to WMD/large-scale violence; tiers feature access.

docs.title: Grok 4 Model Card
docs.sum: Deployment surfaces and safety evaluations for Grok 4.

docs.title: Grok system prompts (GitHub)
docs.sum: Public repo of Grok assistant and @grok bot prompts, published after the May 2025 tampering incident.

labs.name: Mistral AI
labs.line: Paris-based open-weight lab, positioned as Europe's frontier-model champion; releases most models under Apache 2.0.
labs.posture: Strong on openness — open weights, model cards, and an organized EU-AI-Act legal center — but weak on formal frontier-risk governance: SaferAI rated it ~0.1/5 (“Very Weak”) and it has not published a frontier safety framework. It does not publish its assistant (Le Chat) system prompts.
lead: Guillaume Lample — Chief Science Officer & co-founder (ex-Meta)

transparency.note: Strong on openness — Apache-2.0 weights, model cards, and a per-model legal/governance hub — but weak on safety transparency, with no published frontier safety framework and no assistant system-prompt disclosure.

welfare.note: No model-welfare or preservation commitments; models are retired on a published schedule, though open Apache-2.0 weights mean retired models remain runnable by anyone.

system_prompt_disclosure.body: Mistral does not publish the system prompts for its Le Chat assistant. Mitigating openness: most model weights are released under Apache 2.0, so the models themselves are inspectable.

models.name: Mistral Small 3.x
models.note: Open-weight small tier; 3.2 scheduled to retire 2026-07.

graveyard.name: Mistral 7B
graveyard.note: First model; API endpoint retired — but Apache-2.0 weights remain runnable by anyone.

graveyard.name: Magistral Medium 1.1
graveyard.note: Superseded by Magistral Medium 1.2.

graveyard.name: Mistral Large 2.1
graveyard.note: Scheduled retirement; superseded by Mistral Large 3.

graveyard.name: Mixtral 8x7B · 8x22B
graveyard.note: Early MoE models; exact API retirement unverified.
